topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my Tuesday afternoon. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet apartment. It was a listing. My childhood home. The address swam before my eyes, a ghost of a memory suddenly materialized in stark, commercial terms. I stared at the photos—the peeling paint on the porch, the overgrown lilac bush that used to shade my window, the familiar crooked line of the fence. A coldness settled deep in my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight that made my hands tremble. I clicked through the details, my fingers moving mechanically, unable to process the reality that this place, once the bedrock of my world, was now available for transaction. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the scent of old wood and damp earth seemed to cling to the air, inescapable.

[story 2]

I almost didn't look at the email. My thumb hovered over the 'delete' icon, a desperate, futile gesture against the sudden, violent clenching in my stomach. Then I saw it: the listing for Elm Street. It was posted by a real estate agent I vaguely recognized from a neighborhood flyer years ago. My jaw tightened so fiercely I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room like a caged animal. Each step echoed hollowly. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass. The sunlight felt abrasive, illuminating every dust mote dancing in the air, each one a tiny, mocking reminder of what was slipping away. A low sound escaped my throat, a ragged, almost inaudible noise, as I gripped the windowsill until my knuckles bleached white.

[story 3]

It took three full minutes for my brain to register the words. 'For Sale.' The phrase landed like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I sat down slowly, the chair protesting under my sudden stillness. My gaze drifted over the photographs, cataloging the decay—the faded wallpaper, the sagging porch swing. A strange, numb sensation washed over me, like being submerged in ice water. I ran a hand through my hair, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. I could feel a pressure building behind my eyes, a tight, internal knot that refused to loosen. I didn't move to close the browser tab. Instead, I just sat there, letting the silence of the room amplify the hollow echo of that single, devastating announcement.
